rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EDUARDO J FRUCTUOSO JR
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 8/27/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Notice must have attached to it the procedures for how to make a remote appearance by Zoom and CourtCall, as well as remote public access, as set out at https://sf.courts.ca.gov/divisions/probate-court. [LRSF 14.1 C.6.] (2) The estate cannot close until the successor-in-interest of the post-deceased heir is determined. An attorney for her "estate" is not a successor-in-interest. Did Maria Elena Garcia Santiago not have a trust? No personal representative has been appointed for her estate? [LRSF 14.35 G.] (3) When an accounting is presented, original bank or brokerage statements must be filed to verify the ending balance(s) shown in the accounting. None appears to have been filed. [LRSF 14.34 D.] See CRC 7.575(b) for computer-generated printouts. (4) Please file the seller"s closing statement to verify the costs of sale. (5) The accounting period reportedly begins on 2/15/24, but the decedent died on 5/14/23. [LRSF 14.34.] (6) There was a creditor's claim filed on 12/12/23 by AT&T Mobility. (7) Receipts must be filed for the prior distribution made without a Court order. (8) Prob. C. Sect. 9202(b) also requires notice if an heir or beneficiary has previously been confined in a prison or facility under the jurisdiction of the Department of Corrections, etc. (9) The accounting attached as Exhibit A to the petition is not a proper Court conformed accounting. Detailed schedules include the date, source or payee, description, and amount of each transaction. (10) There is no schedule of receipts, showing $364.62. (11) If all cash was invested in interest-bearing accounts, where is the interest shown? (12) The real property was sold on 5/13/23? (No gains or losses schedule was part of the incomplete accounting.) Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 8/13/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (Item 4 of an I&A should NOT be completed when a probate referee HAS BEEN appointed. NB: In order for this Court to track closed matters, orders to terminate and distribute an estate must begin with the following language: "Order for Final Distribution." [LRSF 14.35 I.6.b.] All pleadings must show the name and representative capacity, if any, of the party for whom the attorney is appearing, e.g., John Jones, Executor. "Petitioner" or "Respondent" alone is not sufficient. [LRSF 14.15 A.])

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BRUNA LORETTA BUGEJA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer To Proper Venue
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Respondent Ashley Reed's Motion to Transfer to Proper Venue. Respondent's request for judicial notice is GRANTED, pursuant to Evidence Code section 452. Per the Notice of Motion, Respondent seeks an order transferring venue pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 396b and Probate Code section 7051, on grounds that the probate action was not filed in the proper court. The party seeking to transfer venue must overcome the presumption that the plaintiff has selected a proper venue, and must show that the plaintiff's venue selection is not proper under any of the statutory Code of Civil Procedure section 396b grounds. (Fontaine v. Sup. Ct. (2009) 175 CA4th 830, 836.) The verified Petition for Probate of Lost Will and for Letters of Administration, filed in this court on April 1, 2025, lists San Francisco as Decedent's county of residence at time of death. The Motion to transfer venue is DENIED. Respondent has not met the burden of proof on the Motion, and has not overcome the presumption that Petitioner has selected the proper venue for the probate proceedings concerning Decedent Bruna Bugeja's estate. Under Probate Code section 7051, "If the decedent was domiciled in this state at the time of death, the proper county for proceedings concerning administration of the decedent's estate is the county in which the decedent was domiciled, regardless of where the decedent died." "Domicile" for jurisdictional purposes means where the decedent resided with the intent to remain permanently or for an indefinite time, without any fixed purpose to return to a former residence. (See, Estate of Wardani (2022) 82 CA5th 870, 883; Estate of Phillips (1969) 269 CA2d 656, 659.) "The acquisition of a new domicile is generally understood to require an actual change of residence accompanied by the intention to remain either permanently or for an indefinite time without any fixed or certain purpose to return to the former place of abode." (Estate of Phillips, supra, 269 CA2d at 659.) A motion for change of venue must be supported by competent evidence. (See, Lieppman v. Lieber (1986) 180 Cal. App. 3d 914, 919.) The Motion offers evidence that Respondent and her husband drove Decedent and her husband from San Francisco to Respondent's home in Placer County on October 6, 2024 (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 10.) Decedent and her husband were in contract to purchase a residence in Placer County as of October 19, 2024 (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 14, 15; Bernal Decl. par. 8). The same day Decedent and her husband entered a deposit on the purchase contract, October 19, 2024, Respondent's mother-in-law, acting ostensibly in her role as Decedent's bookkeeper, entered a change of address form on behalf of Decedent, listing 363 Harvard Street, San Francisco as Decedent's "old address" and Respondent's address as Decedent's "new address" (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 16).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BRUNA LORETTA BUGEJA
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For The Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] On November 6, 2024, Decedent's husband was discharged from the rehabilitation facility; he and Decedent moved into an apartment at an assisted living community in Placer County, where Decedent's husband "received daily medical and personal assistance, as well as physical therapy." (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 20.) On November 12, 2024, Decedent was admitted to a hospital emergency room in Placer County. Decedent's husband suffered a fall in The Pines apartment on November 16, 2024, and was hospitalized. On November 19, 2024, Decedent's husband died in the hospital. (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 22.) After her husband's death, and apparently after her own discharge from the hospital, Decedent allegedly never returned to The Pines apartment, but returned to Respondent's house, where she had a room. (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 23; Stacey Reed Decl. par 12.) Decedent died in Placer County on January 19, 2025. (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 24.) The record currently before the court is factually distinguishable from In re Foster's Estate (1959) 170 Cal.App.2d 314, cited by Movant. "[T]he question arises whether there is in the record evidence sufficient to justify the conclusion [] that defendant intended to change and make her residence in the county of Sacramento and that her conduct was illustrative of such intention." (Id. at 317.) Despite her final illnesses and death occurring over several months at various locations in Placer County, there is no showing supported by sufficient evidence on the record that Decedent's conduct - and not the conduct of those around her - illustrated the necessary unity of intent and action to permanently change her residence to Placer County, at least after she, allegedly, personally cancelled the purchase contract on October 25, 2024. As the Reply observes, had Decedent's husband not pre-deceased her, there would likely be a different fact pattern to determine where Decedent was domiciled at the time of her death. That hypothetical fact pattern is not before the court, however. On the record before the court, there is no showing supported by sufficient evidence that Decedent was domiciled in Placer County at the time of her death, for the purposes of determining the correct venue for proceedings concerning administration of her estate. Further, the record before the court supports the presumption that Petitioners correctly selected San Francisco as the proper venue for the administration of Decedent's estate. The contested pour-over will Decedent signed on October 21, 2024, in Placer County, states Decedent is "a resident of San Francisco County, California." Decedent's California General Durable Power of Attorney signed October 14, 2024 lists 363 Harvard Street, San Francisco as her address. Decedent's late husband's concurrently signed California General Durable Power of Attorney lists the same San Francisco address. (Sullivan Decl. Exs. A, B.) Several declarations filed both in support of and in opposition to the Motion state Decedent and her husband lived in their house in San Francisco prior to their final illnesses and deaths in Placer County in late 2024 and early 2025 (Ashley Reed Decl. par. 4, 8, 10, 21, 23; Stacey Reed Decl. par. 6, 7, 13; Suggs Decl. par. 4, 5; Bugeja Decl. par. 2, 3; Castagnola Decl. par. 4, 8, 9). The contested trust instrument at issue in related case In the Matter of the Bugeja 2024 Family Trust (PTR-25-308415), executed October 21, 2024, lists under "Schedule C - Trust Property" the real property located at 363 Harvard Street, San Francisco, and three other real properties, none of which are in Placer County. Decedent's death certificate lists the 363 Harvard Street, San Francisco address (Sullivan Decl. Ex. F).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BRUNA LORETTA BUGEJA
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR (Added For The Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] The moving party on this motion to transfer venue has not carried her burden to overcome the presumption that Petitioner selected the proper venue, nor has Movant shown that this court is not the proper venue under Code of Civil Procedure section 396b for proceedings concerning administration of Decedent's estate. Petitioners shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.
